source: Learning Ansible with Rocky - Ansible Basics: Installation on the management server

questions.q: Which repository provides Ansible and is required for both install methods here?
questions.why: Ansible is available from EPEL, which is required whether you then install via dnf or via pip.

questions.q: Which command installs the EPEL repository on Rocky Linux?
questions.why: EPEL is enabled by installing the epel-release package.

questions.q: Install Ansible from EPEL using dnf.
questions.why: With EPEL enabled, dnf install ansible pulls the packaged version.

questions.q: Which command shows the installed Ansible version, config file, and python details?
questions.why: ansible --version prints the core version, config file, module paths, and python version.

questions.q: The docs note Ansible ships "its own version of python". Why does that matter?
options: Python modules must be pip-installed against Ansible's python (e.g. pip3.11), not the system python
System commands can no longer be run
The system python must be uninstalled
It doubles the memory Ansible uses
questions.why: Ansible's python may differ from the system one (e.g. 3.11 vs 3.6.8); install required modules with the matching pip.

questions.q: Why might you install Ansible via pip instead of from EPEL?
options: To get a newer version than the EPEL package offers
Because pip installs are always system-wide
Because pip removes the SSH requirement
questions.why: The EPEL version can be too old; pip lets you choose the version (and the python it runs on).

questions.q: Install Ansible into your user account with pip for python 3.8.
questions.why: pip3.8 install --user ansible installs Ansible under ~/.local for the current user.

questions.q: After a `pip --user` install, where does the ansible executable live (per the docs example)?
questions.why: A --user pip install places executables under the user's ~/.local/bin.
